_readme: Curated MoA descriptions for Phase 2.5's educational MoA cards. Keys must match the 'moa' strings found in app_data/wells.parquet and app_data/compounds.parquet exactly (case-sensitive). Combined labels in the data (e.g. 'A | B') are looked up by splitting on ' | ' and resolving each part separately -- do not add combined keys here. Add new entries as plain {description, pathway, typically_affects, sources} objects; MoAs with no entry fall back to a generic message in the app. Descriptions are written from general pharmacology references (mainly Wikipedia and NCBI/PMC), each cited in 'sources'.

control vehicle.description: This is not a drug treatment -- it is the vehicle (solvent) control, typically DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide), added at the same volume as the test compounds but with no active ingredient. It establishes the baseline, unperturbed phenotype that every other well is compared against. Differences between a compound's wells and control wells suggest a biological effect; differences among control wells instead reflect technical or batch variation.
control vehicle.pathway: None -- this is the assay's negative-control reference condition, not a drug mechanism.
control vehicle.typically_affects: Nothing pharmacologically; defines the unperturbed baseline for comparison.

calcium channel blocker.description: Calcium channel blockers prevent calcium ions from entering cells through voltage-gated calcium channels in smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and other excitable cells. Because intracellular calcium triggers muscle contraction via proteins like calmodulin and troponin, blocking its entry relaxes smooth muscle and reduces the force and rate of heart contractions. Clinically these drugs treat high blood pressure, angina, and abnormal heart rhythms.
calcium channel blocker.pathway: Voltage-gated calcium channel signaling in excitable cells.
calcium channel blocker.typically_affects: Muscle contraction, vascular tone, and cardiac electrical activity.

CDK inhibitor.description: Cyclin-dependent kinase (CDK) inhibitors block the CDK enzymes that, together with cyclin proteins, drive cells through the phases of the division cycle. Inhibiting CDK4/6 in particular halts the transition from G1 to S phase, stopping cells from replicating their DNA. Several CDK4/6 inhibitors (e.g. palbociclib) are approved cancer therapies used to slow tumor cell proliferation.
CDK inhibitor.pathway: Cell-cycle regulation via the CDK-cyclin-Rb pathway (G1 to S transition).

HSP inhibitor.description: Heat shock proteins (most often HSP90 in this class) are molecular chaperones that fold and stabilize a large set of 'client' proteins many cancer cells depend on for growth and survival. HSP inhibitors bind the chaperone's ATP-binding pocket and block its activity, causing client proteins to misfold and be degraded. This can selectively stress cancer cells that over-rely on chaperone support to stabilize mutated or overexpressed signaling proteins.
HSP inhibitor.pathway: Protein folding / chaperone quality control (HSP ATPase cycle).
HSP inhibitor.typically_affects: Stability of signaling and oncogenic client proteins; broad proteostasis stress.

SRC inhibitor.description: Src is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase that relays signals from cell-surface receptors to control growth, adhesion, and migration. Src inhibitors block its kinase activity -- some lock the protein in an inactive closed conformation, others compete at its ATP-binding site -- reducing downstream signaling that many cancers rely on for invasion and survival. Src family kinases were among the first oncogene targets identified in cancer research.
SRC inhibitor.pathway: Non-receptor tyrosine kinase signaling downstream of growth-factor and adhesion receptors.
SRC inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell growth, adhesion, and migration signaling.

sodium channel blocker.description: Sodium channel blockers impair the flow of sodium ions through voltage-gated sodium channels, which drive the rising phase of electrical action potentials in neurons and heart muscle. By slowing this depolarization, they reduce the excitability of nerve and cardiac tissue. This mechanism underlies local anesthetics, several anticonvulsants, and Class I antiarrhythmic heart medications.

sphingosine 1-phosphate receptor agonist.description: Sphingosine-1-phosphate (S1P) receptors are G-protein-coupled receptors that respond to the lipid signaling molecule S1P to regulate cell proliferation, migration, and -- notably -- the trafficking of immune cells out of lymph nodes. Drugs acting on these receptors can trap lymphocytes inside lymph nodes, which is the basis for their use as immune-modulating therapies, for example in multiple sclerosis.
sphingosine 1-phosphate receptor agonist.pathway: S1P-GPCR signaling controlling lymphocyte trafficking and vascular/cell biology.
sphingosine 1-phosphate receptor agonist.typically_affects: Immune cell trafficking, vascular and cell-migration behavior.

phosphodiesterase inhibitor.description: Phosphodiesterase (PDE) enzymes break down the intracellular second messengers cAMP and cGMP, which relay signals from many hormone and neurotransmitter receptors. PDE inhibitors block this breakdown, letting cAMP or cGMP accumulate and prolonging the signaling they mediate. Because different PDE subtypes are enriched in different tissues, PDE inhibitors have varied uses -- from relaxing smooth muscle (e.g. PDE5 inhibitors) to modulating cardiac and inflammatory signaling.
phosphodiesterase inhibitor.typically_affects: Smooth muscle tone, cardiac contractility, and inflammatory signaling, depending on the PDE subtype.

HDAC inhibitor.description: Histone deacetylase (HDAC) enzymes remove acetyl groups from histone proteins, compacting chromatin and silencing gene transcription. HDAC inhibitors block this activity, leading to histone hyperacetylation, a more open chromatin state, and increased expression of genes that were previously repressed -- including genes controlling cell growth, differentiation, and programmed cell death. They are used and investigated as cancer therapies and, at lower doses, have a long history as mood stabilizers and anti-epileptics.
HDAC inhibitor.typically_affects: Gene expression broadly; often cell-cycle arrest, differentiation, or apoptosis in cancer cells.

PPAR receptor antagonist.description: Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors (PPAR-alpha, -delta, -gamma) are nuclear receptors that act as transcription factors controlling genes involved in lipid and glucose metabolism, energy balance, and inflammation. PPAR antagonists block these receptors rather than activating them; blocking PPAR-gamma in particular has shown antiproliferative effects on certain cancer and blood cell lines in research settings. PPAR agonists (the opposite mechanism) are far better known clinically, e.g. as diabetes and lipid-lowering drugs.
PPAR receptor antagonist.pathway: Nuclear receptor transcriptional regulation of lipid/glucose metabolism genes.
PPAR receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Lipid and glucose metabolism, cell differentiation, and -- for some antagonists -- cancer cell proliferation.

histone lysine methyltransferase inhibit.description: Histone lysine methyltransferases add methyl groups to specific lysine residues on histone tails, a chemical mark that can activate or repress nearby genes depending on which residue is modified. EZH2, one of the best-studied members of this enzyme family, methylates histone H3 at lysine 27 to repress transcription and is frequently overactive in cancers. Inhibitors block the enzyme's catalytic domain, aiming to reverse this repression and restore normal expression of tumor-suppressor genes.
histone lysine methyltransferase inhibit.pathway: Epigenetic regulation via histone lysine methylation (e.g. H3K27 methylation by EZH2).
histone lysine methyltransferase inhibit.typically_affects: Gene expression through chromatin state; often tumor-suppressor gene silencing in cancer.

CDC inhibitor.description: 'CDC inhibitor' in this dataset most often refers to compounds targeting the CDC25 family of phosphatases (CDC25A/B/C), which remove inhibitory phosphate marks from CDK enzymes to allow cell-cycle transitions to proceed. Blocking CDC25 keeps CDKs in their inactive, phosphorylated state, stalling the cell cycle at checkpoints such as the G1/S or G2/M transition. This is being explored therapeutically as a way to arrest the division of cancer cells.
CDC inhibitor.pathway: Cell-cycle checkpoint control via CDC25 phosphatase / CDK activation.

sources.title: Therapeutic Targeting of the CDC25 Phosphatases in Human Acute Myeloid Leukemia (PMC)

potassium channel blocker.description: Potassium channel blockers impede the outward flow of potassium ions that normally repolarizes cells after an electrical impulse. By slowing repolarization, they prolong the action potential and refractory period in cardiac and neuronal tissue. This is the basis of Class III antiarrhythmic heart drugs, and of experimental treatments for conditions like multiple sclerosis where nerve conduction is impaired.
potassium channel blocker.typically_affects: Cardiac and neuronal action potential duration.

GABA receptor antagonist.description: GABA is the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter, and GABA-A receptors are chloride channels that open in response to GABA to dampen neuronal firing. GABA receptor antagonists block this inhibitory chloride current, removing the brain's 'brakes' and producing stimulant or convulsant effects. Because of this, they are mainly studied as tools for counteracting sedative overdoses rather than as standalone therapeutics.
GABA receptor antagonist.pathway: GABAergic inhibitory neurotransmission (GABA-A chloride channel signaling).
GABA receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Neuronal excitability and inhibitory tone in the nervous system.

glutamate receptor antagonist.description: Glutamate is the brain's main excitatory neurotransmitter, acting through receptor types including NMDA, AMPA, and kainate receptors. Glutamate receptor antagonists block these receptors -- for example, NMDA antagonists prevent glutamate from binding or block the channel through other sites -- reducing excitatory signaling. This can protect neurons from excitotoxicity, a form of damage caused by excessive glutamate stimulation.
glutamate receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Neuronal excitability; protection against excitotoxic damage.

sources.title: Excitatory amino acid receptor antagonist - Wikipedia

glucocorticoid receptor agonist.description: Glucocorticoid receptors are cytoplasmic receptors that, once bound by a glucocorticoid hormone (or a synthetic mimic like dexamethasone), move to the nucleus and act as a transcription factor, turning target genes up or down. This broadly suppresses inflammatory gene expression and is the basis for corticosteroid anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive drugs. Because the receptor sits upstream of a large gene-regulatory network, its activation affects metabolism, immune function, and stress response simultaneously.
glucocorticoid receptor agonist.pathway: Nuclear hormone receptor signaling / glucocorticoid-responsive gene transcription.
glucocorticoid receptor agonist.typically_affects: Inflammatory and immune gene expression; metabolic and stress-response genes.

tubulin polymerization inhibitor.description: Tubulin polymerization inhibitors interfere with the assembly of tubulin subunits into microtubules, the structural filaments cells use to build their internal scaffold and separate chromosomes during mitosis. Compounds like colchicine and the vinca alkaloids bind tubulin at specific sites and prevent it from polymerizing, destabilizing the microtubule network. Because dividing cells depend heavily on microtubules to complete mitosis, this mechanism is widely exploited by cancer chemotherapies to halt proliferation.
tubulin polymerization inhibitor.pathway: Microtubule dynamics (tubulin polymerization) in the cytoskeleton and mitotic spindle.

sources.title: Discovery and development of tubulin inhibitors - Wikipedia

cyclooxygenase inhibitor.description: Cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes convert arachidonic acid into prostaglandins, lipid messengers that drive inflammation, pain, and fever. Cyclooxygenase inhibitors -- the mechanism behind NSAIDs such as aspirin and ibuprofen -- block COX-1 and/or COX-2 to reduce prostaglandin production. Selective COX-2 inhibitors were developed to retain the anti-inflammatory effect while sparing COX-1's role in protecting the stomach lining.

protein synthesis inhibitor.description: Protein synthesis inhibitors disrupt the ribosome's ability to translate messenger RNA into protein, typically by binding a ribosomal subunit and blocking a specific step such as tRNA binding, peptide bond formation, or ribosome translocation. Many antibiotics exploit structural differences between bacterial and human ribosomes to selectively kill bacteria while sparing human cells. Non-selective inhibitors, however, can also broadly suppress translation in the treated human cells themselves.
protein synthesis inhibitor.typically_affects: Cellular protein production broadly; bacterial protein synthesis for antibiotic classes.

benzodiazepine receptor agonist.description: Benzodiazepines act at a specific site on GABA-A receptors, distinct from where GABA itself binds, functioning as positive allosteric modulators rather than direct activators. Binding here increases the receptor's affinity for GABA, enhancing the inhibitory chloride current GABA produces, without opening the channel on their own. This produces the sedative, anti-anxiety, and anticonvulsant effects benzodiazepine drugs are used for clinically.
benzodiazepine receptor agonist.pathway: Allosteric modulation of GABA-A receptor chloride channels.
benzodiazepine receptor agonist.typically_affects: Neuronal inhibition; sedation, anxiolysis, and seizure threshold.

sources.title: GABAA receptor positive allosteric modulator - Wikipedia

p21 activated kinase inhibitor.description: p21-activated kinases (PAKs) are effector enzymes of the small GTPases Cdc42 and Rac1, relaying signals that control cytoskeletal organization, cell shape, and cell survival. PAK inhibitors block this kinase activity, which can disrupt cancer cell migration and invasion pathways that depend on PAK signaling. Because PAKs have large, flexible ATP-binding sites, developing selective inhibitors has historically been difficult, and few have progressed far in clinical development.
p21 activated kinase inhibitor.pathway: Rho-family GTPase (Cdc42/Rac) signaling to the cytoskeleton.
p21 activated kinase inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell shape, migration, and cytoskeletal organization.

prostanoid receptor antagonist.description: Prostanoids (prostaglandins and thromboxane) are lipid signaling molecules derived from arachidonic acid that act on a family of dedicated G-protein-coupled receptors (e.g. EP1-4, DP, FP, IP, TP) to mediate inflammation, pain, vasodilation or constriction, and platelet activity. Prostanoid receptor antagonists block one or more of these receptor subtypes directly, rather than blocking prostanoid synthesis the way COX inhibitors do. Because each receptor subtype has a distinct physiological role, selective antagonists are being developed for specific conditions -- for example, thromboxane (TP) receptor antagonists for pulmonary hypertension.
prostanoid receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Inflammation, vascular tone, and platelet activity, depending on receptor subtype.

sources.title: Prostanoid receptors -- IUPHAR/BPS Guide to Pharmacology

adrenergic receptor antagonist.description: Adrenergic receptors respond to the hormones/neurotransmitters adrenaline and noradrenaline, and are divided into alpha (a1, a2) and beta (b1, b2, b3) subtypes with different tissue distributions and effects. Adrenergic receptor antagonists ('blockers') bind these receptors without activating them, preventing adrenaline/noradrenaline from doing so. Beta blockers are widely used for heart rhythm and blood pressure control, while alpha blockers are used for hypertension and benign prostatic hyperplasia.
adrenergic receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Heart rate, blood vessel tone, and smooth muscle contraction, depending on receptor subtype.

adrenergic receptor agonist.description: Adrenergic receptor agonists mimic adrenaline and noradrenaline, activating alpha or beta adrenergic receptors directly. Depending on the receptor subtype engaged, this triggers different intracellular signaling cascades -- a1 receptors increase protein kinase C activity, a2 receptors decrease cAMP/PKA signaling, and b receptors increase cAMP/PKA signaling. Physiologically, this reproduces 'fight-or-flight' effects such as increased heart rate, airway dilation, or blood vessel constriction, depending on which receptors are activated.
adrenergic receptor agonist.typically_affects: Heart rate, airway and blood vessel tone, and other sympathetic ('fight-or-flight') responses.

ALK tyrosine kinase receptor inhibitor.description: Anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) is a receptor tyrosine kinase that, when abnormally fused to other genes (as in certain lung cancers and lymphomas), becomes constitutively active and drives uncontrolled cell growth. ALK inhibitors bind the ATP pocket of the kinase domain, blocking its access to energy and shutting down its signaling. Drugs like crizotinib and alectinib target ALK-rearranged tumors specifically, sparing cells without the fusion.
ALK tyrosine kinase receptor inhibitor.pathway: Receptor tyrosine kinase signaling (ALK fusion oncoprotein activity).
ALK tyrosine kinase receptor inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell growth signaling in ALK-driven cancers.

calcium-activated potassium channel acti.description: BK channels ('big potassium' channels) are potassium channels activated by both membrane depolarization and rising intracellular calcium, forming a negative-feedback loop that links calcium signaling to membrane repolarization. Activators of these channels increase potassium outflow, which hyperpolarizes the cell and dampens excitability -- relevant to smooth muscle tone, neuronal firing, and hormone secretion. This mechanism is being explored for conditions like hypertension and overactive bladder, where reducing smooth muscle excitability is beneficial.
calcium-activated potassium channel acti.typically_affects: Smooth muscle tone, neuronal excitability, and hormone secretion.

endothelin receptor antagonist.description: Endothelin is a potent vasoconstrictor peptide that signals through two receptor subtypes: ETA (mainly on smooth muscle, promoting vasoconstriction and proliferation) and ETB (on smooth muscle and vascular endothelium). Endothelin receptor antagonists competitively block one or both subtypes, preventing endothelin from binding and triggering its signaling cascade. This produces vasodilation and reduced cell proliferation, and is used clinically to treat pulmonary arterial hypertension.
endothelin receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Vascular tone and smooth muscle cell proliferation.

acetylcholine receptor agonist.description: Acetylcholine receptors come in two structurally distinct families: ligand-gated ion channel nicotinic receptors and G-protein-coupled muscarinic receptors. Acetylcholine receptor agonists activate one or both receptor types, mimicking the neurotransmitter acetylcholine to open ion channels (nicotinic) or trigger second-messenger signaling (muscarinic). This drives cholinergic effects throughout the nervous system and at the neuromuscular junction, including muscle contraction and altered neuronal firing.
acetylcholine receptor agonist.pathway: Cholinergic neurotransmission (nicotinic and muscarinic receptor signaling).
acetylcholine receptor agonist.typically_affects: Neuromuscular signaling and autonomic nervous system activity.

HMGCR inhibitor.description: HMG-CoA reductase catalyzes the rate-limiting step of cholesterol biosynthesis in the liver. HMGCR inhibitors (statins) occupy the enzyme's substrate-binding pocket, competitively blocking this step and lowering the cell's internal cholesterol supply. In the liver, this triggers increased uptake of LDL cholesterol from the blood, which is why statins are widely prescribed to lower blood cholesterol and cardiovascular risk.

p38 MAPK inhibitor.description: p38 mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) is activated by cellular stress and inflammatory signals, and in turn phosphorylates downstream targets that regulate inflammatory cytokine production and stress responses. p38 inhibitors typically compete at the kinase's ATP-binding pocket, blocking its ability to phosphorylate these substrates. Early inhibitors showed promise for inflammatory disease but also toxicity issues, since p38 signaling is involved in many normal cellular stress responses as well.
p38 MAPK inhibitor.typically_affects: Inflammatory cytokine production and cellular stress responses.

histamine receptor antagonist.description: Histamine acts on four receptor subtypes (H1-H4); H1 receptor antagonists -- commonly called antihistamines -- occupy the H1 receptor and act as inverse agonists, reducing its baseline signaling rather than simply blocking histamine. This dampens the classic allergic response: capillary permeability, smooth muscle contraction in the airways and gut, and the itch/flare reaction to allergens. H1 antihistamines are the standard treatment for allergy symptoms such as those from pollen or dust exposure.
histamine receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Allergic and inflammatory responses (vascular permeability, smooth muscle contraction, itch).

11-beta hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase inh.description: 11-beta hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 1 (11b-HSD1) converts inactive cortisone into active cortisol inside tissues such as liver and fat, amplifying local glucocorticoid signaling beyond what circulating cortisol alone would produce. Inhibitors block this local activation without touching adrenal cortisol output, and are studied for metabolic syndrome, type 2 diabetes, and obesity by reducing hepatic glucose output and adipose glucocorticoid signaling.
11-beta hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase inh.typically_affects: Hepatic glucose production and adipose tissue glucocorticoid signaling.

AKT inhibitor.description: AKT (protein kinase B) is a central node of the PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway, phosphorylating downstream substrates that promote cell survival, growth, and metabolism. AKT inhibitors block its kinase activity at the ATP-binding site or an allosteric pocket, shutting down this pro-survival signaling in tumors where the pathway is hyperactive -- one of the most frequently altered pathways in human cancer.
AKT inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell survival, growth, and metabolic signaling.

ATM kinase inhibitor.description: ATM (ataxia-telangiectasia mutated) is a master kinase activated by DNA double-strand breaks, phosphorylating downstream effectors such as p53 and CHK2 to trigger cell-cycle arrest and DNA repair. ATM inhibitors block this damage-response signaling, which can sensitize cancer cells to radiotherapy or DNA-damaging chemotherapy by preventing them from pausing to repair damage. Loss-of-function ATM mutations cause the inherited disorder ataxia-telangiectasia.
ATM kinase inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell-cycle checkpoint arrest and DNA repair in response to genotoxic stress.

ATP channel blocker.description: This label refers to ATP-sensitive potassium (KATP) channels, which couple a cell's metabolic state (its ATP/ADP ratio) to its electrical excitability. In pancreatic beta cells, blocking KATP channels -- as sulfonylurea drugs do -- depolarizes the membrane, opens voltage-gated calcium channels, and triggers insulin secretion, which is why these blockers are used to treat type 2 diabetes.
ATP channel blocker.typically_affects: Insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells; smooth muscle and neuronal excitability elsewhere.

Aurora kinase inhibitor.description: Aurora kinases (A, B, C) are essential regulators of mitosis, controlling centrosome maturation, spindle assembly, and chromosome segregation. Inhibitors block their kinase activity, causing mitotic errors such as failed cytokinesis and chromosome missegregation that can trigger death in rapidly dividing cancer cells.
Aurora kinase inhibitor.pathway: Mitotic kinase signaling (spindle assembly and chromosome segregation).

BAX activator.description: BAX is a pro-apoptotic protein normally held inactive in the cytosol; upon activation it inserts into the mitochondrial outer membrane, oligomerizes, and permeabilizes it, releasing cytochrome c and triggering apoptosis. Small-molecule BAX activators bind its N-terminal 'trigger site' to directly force this conformational change, offering a way to kill cancer cells that have blocked apoptosis through other means such as BCL-2 overexpression.
BAX activator.typically_affects: Programmed cell death via mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization.

Bcr-Abl kinase inhibitor.description: The BCR-ABL fusion protein, created by the 'Philadelphia chromosome' translocation, is a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives chronic myeloid leukemia. BCR-ABL inhibitors like imatinib occupy the kinase's ATP-binding pocket, shutting down its signaling and inducing remission in most patients -- one of the first and most successful examples of targeted cancer therapy.
Bcr-Abl kinase inhibitor.pathway: Constitutive tyrosine kinase signaling from the BCR-ABL fusion oncoprotein.
Bcr-Abl kinase inhibitor.typically_affects: Proliferation and survival of BCR-ABL-driven leukemia cells.

Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor.description: BTK is a signaling enzyme downstream of the B-cell receptor that is required for B-cell development, survival, and proliferation. BTK inhibitors such as ibrutinib covalently bind a cysteine in its active site, blocking B-cell receptor signaling, and are used to treat B-cell malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Loss-of-function BTK mutations cause the inherited immunodeficiency X-linked agammaglobulinemia.

CHK inhibitor.description: Checkpoint kinases CHK1 and CHK2 act downstream of ATR/ATM in the DNA damage response, enforcing cell-cycle arrest -- particularly at the G2/M checkpoint -- to allow time for DNA repair. CHK inhibitors override this checkpoint, forcing damaged cells to keep dividing with unrepaired DNA, a strategy used to sensitize cancer cells to DNA-damaging chemotherapy or radiation.
CHK inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell-cycle checkpoint arrest in response to DNA damage.

CLK inhibitor.description: CDC2-like kinases (CLKs) phosphorylate SR proteins, a family of splicing factors, controlling how pre-mRNA is spliced into different exon combinations. CLK inhibitors block this phosphorylation, disrupting normal alternative splicing patterns -- an approach being explored to selectively kill cancer cells that depend on specific splicing programs. This is a less mature drug class studied mainly in research and early oncology programs rather than approved therapeutics.
CLK inhibitor.pathway: Pre-mRNA alternative splicing regulation (SR protein phosphorylation).
CLK inhibitor.typically_affects: RNA splicing patterns and, downstream, cell growth/survival in splicing-dependent cancers.

sources.title: Inhibitors of CLK Protein Kinases Suppress Cell Growth and Induce Apoptosis by Modulating Pre-mRNA Splicing (PMC)

DNA alkylating agent.description: Alkylating agents attach reactive alkyl groups directly onto DNA bases, commonly guanine, forming adducts and cross-links that distort the double helix and block DNA replication and transcription. Because rapidly dividing cells are especially vulnerable to this damage, alkylating agents such as cyclophosphamide are among the oldest and most widely used classes of cancer chemotherapy.
DNA alkylating agent.typically_affects: DNA replication and transcription; triggers apoptosis in dividing cells.

DNA directed RNA polymerase inhibitor.description: RNA polymerase reads a DNA template to synthesize RNA, the essential first step of gene expression. Inhibitors of this enzyme block transcription broadly; because bacterial and human RNA polymerases differ structurally, selective inhibitors like rifampicin can shut down bacterial transcription (as an antibiotic) while sparing human cells.
DNA directed RNA polymerase inhibitor.typically_affects: Gene transcription broadly; bacterial transcription for antibiotic-class inhibitors.

DNA methyltransferase inhibitor.description: DNA methyltransferases (DNMTs) add methyl groups to cytosine bases in DNA, an epigenetic mark that typically silences nearby genes. Inhibitors like azacitidine and decitabine get incorporated into DNA in place of cytosine and trap DNMT enzymes, depleting methylation and reactivating silenced tumor-suppressor genes -- a mechanism used in myelodysplastic syndrome and leukemia treatment.
DNA methyltransferase inhibitor.typically_affects: Gene expression through DNA methylation-dependent silencing.

DNA polymerase inhibitor.description: DNA polymerases synthesize new DNA strands during replication and repair by extending a primer using a template strand. Inhibitors block this process either by occupying the enzyme's active site directly or, more commonly, by acting as chain-terminating nucleoside analogs that get incorporated into the growing strand and stop further extension -- the basis for many antiviral and anticancer nucleoside drugs.
DNA polymerase inhibitor.typically_affects: DNA replication in dividing cells or replicating viruses.

DNA synthesis inhibitor.description: This is a broad category covering any compound that blocks the production of new DNA, whether by inhibiting the enzymes of replication directly, starving cells of nucleotide building blocks, or damaging the DNA template itself. Because DNA synthesis is required for cell division, these agents are widely used as anticancer chemotherapy and, for pathogen-selective versions, as antimicrobials.
DNA synthesis inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell proliferation (S-phase of the cell cycle).

DYRK inhibitor.description: Dual-specificity tyrosine-phosphorylation-regulated kinases (DYRKs), especially DYRK1A, phosphorylate substrates involved in cell differentiation, proliferation, and neuronal development. DYRK1A lies in the Down syndrome critical region of chromosome 21, and its overexpression is implicated in the neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative features of Down syndrome and Alzheimer's disease, making DYRK inhibitors a research target for both.
DYRK inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell differentiation, proliferation, and neuronal development/function.

EGFR inhibitor.description: The epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) is a receptor tyrosine kinase that, when bound by growth factors like EGF, dimerizes and autophosphorylates to activate downstream RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT proliferation pathways. EGFR inhibitors -- small-molecule kinase inhibitors like gefitinib or antibodies like cetuximab -- block this activation and are widely used against EGFR-mutant or EGFR-overexpressing cancers such as non-small-cell lung cancer.

FAAH inhibitor.description: Fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) breaks down anandamide, an endogenous cannabinoid ('endocannabinoid') that activates the same receptors as THC. FAAH inhibitors block this breakdown, raising anandamide levels and prolonging its analgesic and mood-related effects -- an approach explored for pain and anxiety that, unlike direct cannabinoid agonists, doesn't directly activate the receptor itself.
FAAH inhibitor.typically_affects: Pain, mood, and anxiety-related endocannabinoid signaling.

FGFR inhibitor.description: Fibroblast growth factor receptors (FGFR1-4) are receptor tyrosine kinases that drive cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and tissue development in response to FGF ligands. FGFR gene amplifications, mutations, and fusions occur in several cancers, and FGFR inhibitors block the receptor's kinase activity to shut down this growth signaling.

FLT3 inhibitor.description: FMS-like tyrosine kinase 3 (FLT3) is a receptor tyrosine kinase normally expressed on blood stem/progenitor cells; activating FLT3 mutations, especially internal tandem duplications, are common drivers of acute myeloid leukemia (AML). FLT3 inhibitors block this constitutively active signaling, reducing leukemic cell proliferation in FLT3-mutant AML.
FLT3 inhibitor.typically_affects: Proliferation of hematopoietic and leukemic cells.

FOXM1 inhibitor.description: FOXM1 is a transcription factor that drives expression of genes required for cell-cycle progression, particularly the G2/M transition, and is frequently overactive in cancer, where it's associated with proliferation, chemoresistance, and poor prognosis. Because FOXM1 lacks an easily druggable enzymatic pocket, most inhibitors work indirectly, blocking its DNA binding or promoting its degradation rather than occupying a classic active site.
FOXM1 inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell-cycle progression and, in cancer, chemoresistance.

sources.title: Regulation of the master regulator FOXM1 in cancer (PMC)

G protein signaling inhibitor.description: G proteins relay signals from G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) to intracellular effectors like adenylyl cyclase and phospholipase C, controlling an enormous range of physiological responses. Inhibitors of G-protein signaling block this relay at the level of the G protein itself, rather than the receptor, dampening downstream second-messenger signaling regardless of which GPCR activated it.
G protein signaling inhibitor.pathway: Heterotrimeric G-protein signal transduction downstream of GPCRs.
G protein signaling inhibitor.typically_affects: Second-messenger signaling (cAMP, calcium, etc.) broadly across G-protein-coupled pathways.

ICAM1 expression inhibitor.description: Intercellular adhesion molecule 1 (ICAM-1) is displayed on the surface of activated endothelial cells during inflammation, where it binds integrins on circulating leukocytes to anchor them before they migrate into tissue. Compounds that inhibit ICAM-1 expression reduce this adhesion step, dampening leukocyte recruitment and the resulting inflammatory response.
ICAM1 expression inhibitor.typically_affects: Leukocyte adhesion and migration into inflamed tissue.

IGF-1 inhibitor.description: Insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) signals through the IGF-1 receptor, a receptor tyrosine kinase that activates PI3K-AKT and RAS-MAPK pathways to promote cell growth and survival, closely related to insulin signaling. IGF-1 pathway inhibitors block this receptor or its ligand, an approach studied in cancers that rely on IGF-1 signaling for growth, and in some growth-related endocrine disorders.

JAK inhibitor.description: Janus kinases (JAK1-3, TYK2) are enzymes that associate with cytokine receptors and, upon activation, phosphorylate STAT transcription factors to switch on cytokine-responsive genes (the JAK-STAT pathway). JAK inhibitors ('jakinibs') block this signaling and are used clinically to treat autoimmune and inflammatory diseases like rheumatoid arthritis by dampening cytokine-driven immune activation.
JAK inhibitor.typically_affects: Immune cell activation and cytokine-driven gene expression.

JNK inhibitor.description: c-Jun N-terminal kinases (JNKs) are stress-activated MAP kinases that respond to cytokines, UV radiation, and other cellular stress by phosphorylating transcription factors like c-Jun, influencing inflammation, apoptosis, and stress responses. JNK inhibitors block this stress-signaling cascade, an approach studied for inflammatory diseases and for protecting cells from stress-induced apoptosis.

KIT inhibitor.description: KIT (CD117) is a receptor tyrosine kinase for stem cell factor, important for the development of blood cells, mast cells, melanocytes, and the interstitial cells of Cajal in the gut. Activating KIT mutations drive gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GISTs) and some leukemias/mastocytosis, and KIT inhibitors like imatinib block this signaling to treat these conditions.
KIT inhibitor.pathway: KIT (stem cell factor receptor) tyrosine kinase signaling.
KIT inhibitor.typically_affects: Proliferation of KIT-driven tumor cells; mast cell and hematopoietic signaling.

LDL oxidation inhibitor.description: Oxidized LDL cholesterol, rather than native LDL, is thought to be a key driver of atherosclerosis, since it's taken up unchecked by macrophages to form the foam cells that build arterial plaques. LDL oxidation inhibitors, often antioxidants, block this oxidative modification, an approach investigated for slowing atherosclerotic plaque formation.
LDL oxidation inhibitor.pathway: Lipid peroxidation / oxidative modification of LDL particles.
LDL oxidation inhibitor.typically_affects: Atherosclerotic plaque formation via macrophage foam-cell uptake of oxidized LDL.

MEK inhibitor.description: MEK1/2 are kinases that sit directly downstream of RAF in the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK (MAPK) pathway, phosphorylating and activating ERK to drive cell proliferation. MEK inhibitors like trametinib block this step and are used, often alongside RAF inhibitors, in cancers with hyperactive MAPK signaling such as BRAF-mutant melanoma.

MER tyrosine kinase inhibitor.description: MER (MERTK) is a receptor tyrosine kinase of the TAM family (TYRO3, AXL, MERTK) that helps macrophages clear apoptotic cells and can promote an immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment when co-opted by cancer cells. MERTK inhibitors block this signaling, an approach studied both to boost anti-tumor immunity and to directly slow proliferation of MERTK-dependent cancer cells.
MER tyrosine kinase inhibitor.pathway: TAM family (MERTK) receptor tyrosine kinase signaling.
MER tyrosine kinase inhibitor.typically_affects: Efferocytosis (apoptotic cell clearance) and tumor immune evasion signaling.

NFkB pathway inhibitor.description: NF-kB is a transcription factor family that, once released from its inhibitory partner IkB, moves to the nucleus and switches on genes for inflammatory cytokines, cell survival, and immune responses. NF-kB pathway inhibitors block this activation at various steps, such as IkB kinase inhibition, reducing inflammatory gene expression -- relevant to inflammatory disease and to cancers that rely on NF-kB for survival.
NFkB pathway inhibitor.typically_affects: Inflammatory gene expression and cell survival signaling.

PDGFR tyrosine kinase receptor inhibitor.description: Platelet-derived growth factor receptors (PDGFR-alpha/beta) are receptor tyrosine kinases that drive proliferation of connective tissue cells, smooth muscle, and, in disease, certain cancers and fibrotic tissue. PDGFR inhibitors block this signaling and are used in GIST and certain leukemias with PDGFR fusions, and are being explored for fibrotic diseases.
PDGFR tyrosine kinase receptor inhibitor.typically_affects: Proliferation of mesenchymal cells; fibrosis and certain cancers.

PI3K inhibitor.description: Phosphoinositide 3-kinase (PI3K) phosphorylates membrane lipids to generate PIP3, recruiting and activating AKT and other effectors that drive cell growth, survival, and metabolism. PI3K is one of the most commonly mutated/activated pathways in cancer, and PI3K inhibitors block this lipid kinase activity to shut down downstream AKT-mTOR signaling.
PI3K inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell growth, survival, and metabolic signaling.

PKC activator.description: Protein kinase C (PKC) isoforms are activated physiologically by diacylglycerol and calcium downstream of phospholipase C, phosphorylating substrates that regulate cell growth, differentiation, and apoptosis. PKC activators such as phorbol esters and bryostatins mimic this activation directly, and are studied both as cancer-promoting research tools and, in some contexts, as potential therapeutics for conditions like Alzheimer's disease or HIV latency reversal.
PKC activator.typically_affects: Cell growth, differentiation, and apoptosis signaling.

PKC inhibitor.description: PKC inhibitors block the same protein kinase C family, typically at its ATP-binding site, reducing downstream signaling that many cancers and inflammatory conditions rely on for growth and cytokine production. Because different PKC isoforms have different, sometimes opposing, roles in different tissues, isoform selectivity is a major consideration in PKC inhibitor development.
PKC inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell growth, differentiation, and inflammatory signaling.

PLK inhibitor.description: Polo-like kinases, especially PLK1, coordinate multiple steps of mitosis, including centrosome maturation, spindle assembly, and the metaphase-to-anaphase transition. PLK inhibitors block this activity, arresting cells in mitosis and triggering death of rapidly dividing cancer cells that depend heavily on PLK1 for accurate division.

PPAR receptor inverse agonist.description: PPAR inverse agonists bind the same nuclear peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors as PPAR antagonists, but actively suppress the receptor's baseline transcriptional activity below its unliganded level rather than simply blocking agonist binding. This matters because PPARs retain some constitutive activity even without a ligand, so inverse agonists produce a stronger, more directional shutdown of PPAR-driven metabolic and differentiation genes.
PPAR receptor inverse agonist.typically_affects: Lipid/glucose metabolism and adipocyte differentiation genes.

RAF inhibitor.description: RAF kinases (ARAF, BRAF, CRAF) sit at the top of the RAS-RAF-MEK-ERK (MAPK) cascade; the BRAF V600E mutation, found in a large fraction of melanomas, locks RAF in an active state and drives constant downstream signaling. RAF inhibitors like vemurafenib selectively block this mutant-active kinase, shutting down MAPK signaling in BRAF-mutant tumors.
RAF inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell proliferation signaling, particularly in BRAF-mutant cancers.

RAGE receptor antagonist.description: The receptor for advanced glycation end-products (RAGE) is activated by AGEs -- proteins/lipids damaged by chronic high blood sugar -- as well as other danger-associated molecules, triggering NF-kB-driven inflammatory signaling. RAGE antagonists block this receptor, an approach studied for diabetic complications and chronic inflammatory conditions where AGE-RAGE signaling drives tissue damage.
RAGE receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Chronic inflammatory signaling, notably in diabetic tissue damage.

RET tyrosine kinase inhibitor.description: RET is a receptor tyrosine kinase for glial-derived neurotrophic factor family ligands, important for kidney and enteric nervous system development; activating RET mutations/fusions drive some thyroid cancers and non-small-cell lung cancers. RET inhibitors selectively block this kinase's activity in RET-altered tumors.

SIRT inhibitor.description: Sirtuins (SIRT1-7) are NAD+-dependent deacetylase enzymes that remove acetyl groups from histones and other proteins, linking cellular energy status to gene expression, DNA repair, and metabolism. SIRT inhibitors block this deacetylase activity and are studied mainly as anticancer and antiviral tool compounds, in contrast to the better-known SIRT activators explored for aging and metabolic disease.
SIRT inhibitor.typically_affects: Gene expression, DNA repair, and metabolic regulation via protein acetylation state.

T-type calcium channel blocker.description: T-type ('transient') calcium channels open at more negative membrane potentials than the L-type channels targeted by conventional calcium channel blockers, and are important for pacemaker activity in the heart and rhythmic firing in neurons. T-type blockers dampen this low-threshold calcium influx, an approach used for certain arrhythmias and studied for epilepsy and pain.
T-type calcium channel blocker.typically_affects: Cardiac pacemaker activity and neuronal rhythmic firing.

TGF beta receptor inhibitor.description: Transforming growth factor beta (TGF-beta) signals through a receptor serine/threonine kinase complex that activates SMAD transcription factors, regulating cell growth arrest, differentiation, and -- notably -- fibrosis and immune suppression. TGF-beta receptor inhibitors block this signaling, studied both to reduce pathological fibrosis and to counteract the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment in cancer.
TGF beta receptor inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell growth arrest, fibrosis, and immune regulation.

VEGFR inhibitor.description: Vascular endothelial growth factor receptors (VEGFR1-3) on endothelial cells drive angiogenesis, the formation of new blood vessels, in response to VEGF ligands. VEGFR inhibitors block this signaling to starve tumors of their blood supply (anti-angiogenic cancer therapy) and are also used to treat wet age-related macular degeneration by curbing abnormal vessel growth in the eye.

sources.title: Vascular endothelial growth factor receptor - Wikipedia

acetylcholine receptor antagonist.description: Acetylcholine receptor antagonists block either the ligand-gated ion channel nicotinic receptors or the G-protein-coupled muscarinic receptors, preventing acetylcholine from triggering its normal excitatory or modulatory effects. Depending on which receptor family and subtype is targeted, these drugs relax smooth muscle (e.g. anticholinergics for overactive bladder or COPD), block neuromuscular transmission during surgery, or reduce parasympathetic tone.
acetylcholine receptor antagonist.pathway: Cholinergic neurotransmission (nicotinic and/or muscarinic receptor blockade).
acetylcholine receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Neuromuscular transmission and autonomic (parasympathetic) nervous system activity.

adenosine deaminase inhibitor.description: Adenosine deaminase (ADA) breaks down adenosine and deoxyadenosine as part of purine metabolism; inhibiting it, as pentostatin does, causes toxic accumulation of deoxyadenosine nucleotides that is particularly damaging to lymphocytes, which rely heavily on this salvage pathway. This selective lymphotoxicity is exploited to treat certain leukemias, while inherited ADA deficiency causes a severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID).

adenosine receptor agonist.description: Adenosine receptors (A1, A2A, A2B, A3) are G-protein-coupled receptors activated by the nucleoside adenosine, a signal that generally rises during cellular stress or high energy demand and acts to dampen activity and increase blood flow. Adenosine receptor agonists mimic this signal -- A1 agonists slow heart rate and are used to terminate certain arrhythmias, while other subtypes affect vasodilation, inflammation, and sleep drive.
adenosine receptor agonist.typically_affects: Cardiac conduction, vascular tone, inflammation, and sleep drive, depending on receptor subtype.

adenosine receptor antagonist.description: Adenosine receptor antagonists block the same receptor family, preventing adenosine's normally inhibitory effects. Caffeine is the best-known example, antagonizing A1 and A2A receptors in the brain to counteract adenosine's sleep-promoting, arousal-dampening signal, producing the alertness-boosting effect of coffee.
adenosine receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Arousal, sleep drive, and cardiovascular tone, depending on receptor subtype.

adenosine reuptake inhibitor.description: Adenosine's extracellular signaling is terminated in part by reuptake through nucleoside transporters back into cells. Adenosine reuptake inhibitors like dipyridamole block this transporter, raising extracellular adenosine levels and prolonging its effects, including vasodilation and inhibition of platelet aggregation -- the basis for dipyridamole's use as an antiplatelet/vasodilator drug.

alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor.description: Alcohol dehydrogenase is the first enzyme in ethanol metabolism, converting it to the toxic intermediate acetaldehyde. Inhibitors like fomepizole block this enzyme and are used as an antidote for methanol or ethylene glycol poisoning, since the same enzyme would otherwise convert those alcohols into far more toxic metabolites.
alcohol dehydrogenase inhibitor.typically_affects: Metabolism of ingested alcohols into toxic aldehyde metabolites.

aldehyde dehydrogenase activator.description: Aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH2) detoxifies acetaldehyde from alcohol metabolism and other reactive aldehydes generated during oxidative stress, such as those produced during a heart attack. ALDH2 activators like Alda-1 boost this detoxification, an approach studied for reducing tissue damage from ischemia and for a common East Asian ALDH2 gene variant that impairs enzyme activity.
aldehyde dehydrogenase activator.typically_affects: Clearance of toxic aldehydes from alcohol metabolism and oxidative/ischemic stress.

aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitor.description: Aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitors block the same enzyme, most famously in disulfiram, used to treat alcohol use disorder: by preventing acetaldehyde breakdown, disulfiram causes an unpleasant buildup of acetaldehyde -- flushing, nausea -- whenever the patient drinks alcohol, creating a conditioned aversion.

aldose reductase inhibitor.description: Aldose reductase converts excess glucose to sorbitol in cells (the polyol pathway), and under chronic hyperglycemia this sorbitol accumulates and contributes to diabetic nerve, eye, and kidney damage. Aldose reductase inhibitors like epalrestat block this conversion, an approach used and studied to slow diabetic neuropathy and retinopathy.
aldose reductase inhibitor.typically_affects: Diabetic complications in nerve, eye, and kidney tissue.

angiogenesis inhibitor.description: Angiogenesis inhibitors block the formation of new blood vessels by interfering with signaling pathways like VEGF, either directly or through other anti-angiogenic mechanisms. Tumors require new blood vessel growth to grow beyond a small size, so angiogenesis inhibitors are widely used in oncology to starve tumors of nutrients and oxygen, and in ophthalmology to treat vision-threatening abnormal vessel growth.

antioxidant.description: Antioxidants neutralize reactive oxygen species (free radicals) generated by normal metabolism and external stressors, preventing them from damaging DNA, proteins, and lipids. In this dataset, 'antioxidant' is a broad mechanistic label covering compounds that act through this general radical-scavenging or redox-buffering mechanism, rather than a single specific molecular target.
antioxidant.pathway: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging / redox buffering.
antioxidant.typically_affects: Oxidative stress and its downstream damage to DNA, lipids, and proteins.

astrocyte modulating agent.description: Astrocytes are the most abundant glial cell type in the brain, maintaining the blood-brain barrier, supplying neurons with metabolic support, and regulating neurotransmitter levels at synapses via the many receptors and transporters on their surface. 'Astrocyte modulating agent' is a broad label for compounds that act on this astrocyte signaling machinery rather than directly on neurons -- relevant to neurodegenerative and neuropsychiatric research, though the exact molecular target varies by compound.
astrocyte modulating agent.typically_affects: Neuron-glia communication, blood-brain barrier maintenance, and neuronal metabolic support.

bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit inhibito.description: The bacterial ribosome's small (30S) subunit is where mRNA is decoded and aminoacyl-tRNAs are selected during translation. Antibiotics like aminoglycosides bind irreversibly here and cause misreading of the genetic code, while tetracyclines bind reversibly and block tRNA from docking altogether -- both classes exploit structural differences between bacterial and human ribosomes for selective antibacterial activity.
bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit inhibito.typically_affects: Bacterial protein synthesis (translation fidelity and initiation).

bacterial cell wall synthesis inhibitor.description: Bacterial cell walls are built from peptidoglycan, a mesh polymer that gives the cell structural integrity and osmotic protection. Cell wall synthesis inhibitors -- most famously beta-lactam antibiotics like penicillin -- block the enzymes that cross-link peptidoglycan strands, causing the wall to weaken and the bacterium to rupture under its own internal pressure.
bacterial cell wall synthesis inhibitor.typically_affects: Bacterial cell wall integrity and structural stability.

bone morphogenic protein inhibitor.description: Bone morphogenetic proteins (BMPs) are TGF-beta superfamily signaling molecules that, via SMAD transcription factors, regulate bone and cartilage formation as well as broader developmental patterning. BMP inhibitors block this signaling -- of interest both for conditions of excessive bone/vascular calcification, such as fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva, and for research into stem cell differentiation.
bone morphogenic protein inhibitor.typically_affects: Bone and cartilage formation; developmental cell differentiation.

bromodomain inhibitor.description: Bromodomain-containing proteins, especially the BET family (BRD2/3/4/T), 'read' acetylated histone marks and recruit transcriptional machinery to activate nearby genes, including oncogenes like MYC. BET/bromodomain inhibitors such as JQ1 competitively displace these reader proteins from acetylated chromatin, shutting down the oncogenic transcription they support.
bromodomain inhibitor.typically_affects: Transcription of BET-dependent genes, notably oncogenes such as MYC.

c-Met inhibitor.description: c-Met is the receptor tyrosine kinase for hepatocyte growth factor (HGF), driving cell proliferation, motility, and invasion during normal development and wound healing. c-Met gene amplification or mutation drives a subset of cancers, and c-Met inhibitors block this signaling to slow tumor growth and metastasis in MET-altered tumors.

cannabinoid receptor agonist.description: Cannabinoid receptors CB1 (mainly in the brain) and CB2 (mainly on immune cells) are G-protein-coupled receptors normally activated by endocannabinoids like anandamide, as well as plant-derived THC. Cannabinoid receptor agonists mimic this signaling, producing effects on pain, appetite, mood, and inflammation depending on which receptor subtype and tissue is engaged.
cannabinoid receptor agonist.typically_affects: Pain perception, appetite, mood, and immune/inflammatory signaling.

carbonic anhydrase inhibitor.description: Carbonic anhydrase catalyzes the interconversion of carbon dioxide and water into bicarbonate and protons, a reaction central to pH regulation, fluid secretion, and CO2 transport throughout the body. Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors like acetazolamide block this reaction and are used as diuretics, for glaucoma (by reducing aqueous humor production), and for altitude sickness.
carbonic anhydrase inhibitor.typically_affects: Fluid secretion (kidney, eye) and acid-base/CO2 balance.

caspase activator.description: Caspases are proteases that execute apoptosis by cleaving hundreds of cellular substrates once activated in a proteolytic cascade triggered by death receptors or mitochondrial damage. Caspase activators promote this cascade directly, an approach studied for killing cancer cells that have otherwise evaded apoptosis.

caspase inhibitor.description: Caspase inhibitors block the same protease cascade, preventing cells from completing apoptosis. This is studied therapeutically in conditions where excessive cell death drives disease -- for example limiting neuron loss after stroke or liver cell death in acute liver failure -- though broadly blocking apoptosis also carries a cancer risk if used too indiscriminately.

catechol O methyltransferase inhibitor.description: Catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) breaks down catecholamine neurotransmitters, including dopamine, norepinephrine, and levodopa, the dopamine precursor used to treat Parkinson's disease. COMT inhibitors like entacapone block this breakdown, prolonging levodopa's availability to the brain and smoothing out its therapeutic effect in Parkinson's disease.
catechol O methyltransferase inhibitor.typically_affects: Dopamine and levodopa availability in the brain.

chelating agent.description: Chelating agents bind metal ions such as lead, iron, or copper tightly through multiple coordination bonds, forming a stable, water-soluble complex that the body can excrete. This mechanism is the basis for treating heavy-metal poisoning and metal-overload disorders, such as deferoxamine for iron overload or EDTA for lead poisoning.
chelating agent.typically_affects: Systemic levels of free (unbound) metal ions.

chloride channel blocker.description: Chloride channels regulate the flow of chloride ions across cell membranes, contributing to membrane potential, cell volume regulation, and, in epithelial tissue, fluid secretion. Chloride channel blockers interfere with this flow -- relevant, for example, to smooth muscle excitability and to research on cystic fibrosis-related chloride transport.
chloride channel blocker.typically_affects: Membrane excitability, cell volume, and epithelial fluid secretion.

coagulation factor inhibitor.description: The coagulation cascade is a chain of sequentially activated clotting factors (proteases) that ultimately converts fibrinogen into the fibrin mesh of a blood clot. Coagulation factor inhibitors block one of these factors directly -- for example, factor Xa inhibitors like rivaroxaban -- preventing clot formation and are used as anticoagulants to treat or prevent thrombosis.

cyclin D inhibitor.description: Cyclin D partners with CDK4/6 to phosphorylate the retinoblastoma protein (Rb), releasing E2F transcription factors and driving the G1-to-S phase transition of the cell cycle. Cyclin D inhibitors block this complex's activity, functionally overlapping with CDK4/6 inhibitors, halting cell-cycle progression -- a mechanism exploited in hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer treatment.

cytochrome C release inhibitor.description: Cytochrome c is normally sequestered inside mitochondria as part of the electron transport chain; its release into the cytosol, through pores formed by BAX/BAK, is the key trigger of the intrinsic (mitochondrial) apoptosis pathway, activating caspases via the apoptosome. Cytochrome c release inhibitors block this step, protecting cells from mitochondrial apoptosis -- of interest in conditions with excessive cell death, such as neurodegeneration or ischemic injury.
cytochrome C release inhibitor.typically_affects: Programmed cell death via mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization.

cytokine production inhibitor.description: Cytokines are signaling proteins -- interleukins, TNF, interferons -- secreted by immune cells to coordinate inflammation and immune responses. Cytokine production inhibitors block the intracellular signaling or transcriptional machinery needed to synthesize these proteins, dampening inflammation broadly rather than neutralizing a single already-secreted cytokine.

dehydrogenase inhibitor.description: Dehydrogenases are a large enzyme class that catalyze oxidation reactions by removing hydrogen, typically transferring it to a cofactor like NAD+ or FAD, participating in pathways from glycolysis to steroid synthesis to drug metabolism. 'Dehydrogenase inhibitor' is a broad mechanistic label in this dataset covering compounds that block one of these many enzymes, rather than a single specific target.
dehydrogenase inhibitor.typically_affects: Varies by specific dehydrogenase target; broadly, cellular metabolism.

dihydroorotate dehydrogenase inhibitor.description: Dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (DHODH) catalyzes a rate-limiting step in de novo pyrimidine synthesis, which rapidly dividing cells, including activated lymphocytes, depend on heavily to make new DNA and RNA. DHODH inhibitors like leflunomide block this step, suppressing lymphocyte proliferation -- the basis for their use as disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs in autoimmune disease.
dihydroorotate dehydrogenase inhibitor.typically_affects: Proliferation of rapidly dividing cells, notably activated lymphocytes.

discoidin domain receptor inhibitor.description: Discoidin domain receptors (DDR1, DDR2) are unusual receptor tyrosine kinases activated not by soluble growth factors but by binding directly to collagen in the extracellular matrix. DDR inhibitors block this collagen-sensing signaling, which is implicated in fibrosis, atherosclerosis, and cancer cell invasion through collagen-rich tissue.
discoidin domain receptor inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell-extracellular matrix interaction; fibrosis and tumor invasion.

sources.title: Inhibitors of Discoidin Domain Receptor (DDR) Kinases for Cancer and Inflammation (PMC)

dopamine receptor antagonist.description: Dopamine receptors (D1-D5) are G-protein-coupled receptors that mediate dopamine's roles in reward, movement control, and, in the mesolimbic pathway, psychosis. Dopamine receptor antagonists, the core mechanism of antipsychotic drugs, block D2-family receptors to reduce psychotic symptoms, though this same blockade in motor circuits can cause Parkinson-like side effects.
dopamine receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Reward, motor control, and psychosis-related brain circuits.

elastase inhibitor.description: Elastase enzymes break down elastin, the protein that gives lung and connective tissue its elasticity; unchecked elastase activity, such as from neutrophils during inflammation, degrades lung tissue and contributes to emphysema. Elastase inhibitors, including the natural inhibitor alpha-1 antitrypsin, block this degradation to protect tissue elasticity.

enkephalinase inhibitor.description: 'Enkephalinase' is an older name for neprilysin (NEP), a membrane enzyme that degrades the body's own opioid-like peptides (enkephalins) as well as natriuretic peptides. Enkephalinase inhibitors like racecadotril block this breakdown in the gut, prolonging enkephalin activity there to reduce intestinal fluid secretion -- the basis for their use as antidiarrheal drugs.
enkephalinase inhibitor.pathway: Enkephalin (endogenous opioid peptide) metabolism via neprilysin.
enkephalinase inhibitor.typically_affects: Intestinal fluid secretion; local opioid peptide signaling.

sources.title: A Comprehensive Review of the Pharmacodynamics, Pharmacokinetics, and Clinical Effects of Racecadotril (PMC)

estrogen receptor antagonist.description: Estrogen receptors are nuclear hormone receptors that, once bound by estrogen, drive transcription of genes involved in reproductive tissue growth, including estrogen-sensitive breast cancers. Estrogen receptor antagonists such as tamoxifen and fulvestrant block or degrade this receptor, slowing growth of hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer, and are also used for other estrogen-dependent conditions.
estrogen receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Growth of estrogen-sensitive reproductive tissue, including breast cancer.

farnesyltransferase inhibitor.description: Farnesyltransferase attaches a lipid 'farnesyl' anchor to certain proteins, including RAS, which is required for RAS to localize to the cell membrane and signal properly. Farnesyltransferase inhibitors block this modification, an approach originally developed to indirectly target RAS-driven cancers, and are used in the rare disease Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome.
farnesyltransferase inhibitor.typically_affects: Membrane localization and signaling of farnesylated proteins.

focal adhesion kinase inhibitor.description: Focal adhesion kinase (FAK) is a non-receptor tyrosine kinase that relays signals from integrins, the cell-matrix adhesion receptors, coordinating cell migration, invasion, and survival. FAK inhibitors block this signaling, an approach studied to reduce cancer cell migration and metastasis, as well as fibrosis.
focal adhesion kinase inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell migration, invasion, and adhesion-dependent survival.

free fatty acid receptor agonist.description: Free fatty acid receptor 1 (FFAR1/GPR40) is a G-protein-coupled receptor on pancreatic beta cells activated by circulating long-chain fatty acids, amplifying glucose-stimulated insulin secretion. FFAR1 agonists mimic this signal to boost insulin release only when glucose is already elevated, a 'glucose-dependent' safety feature that reduces the hypoglycemia risk seen with older insulin secretagogues.
free fatty acid receptor agonist.typically_affects: Glucose-stimulated insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells.

sources.title: Free fatty acid receptors as therapeutic targets for the treatment of diabetes (PMC)

glucosidase inhibitor.description: Alpha-glucosidase enzymes in the intestinal brush border break complex carbohydrates down into absorbable simple sugars. Glucosidase inhibitors like acarbose block this final digestive step, slowing carbohydrate absorption and blunting the post-meal blood sugar spike in type 2 diabetes.

glutamate receptor agonist.description: Glutamate receptor agonists activate the brain's principal excitatory neurotransmitter receptors -- NMDA, AMPA, kainate, and metabotropic subtypes -- driving or enhancing excitatory neurotransmission. These are used mainly as research tools to probe excitatory signaling, since directly boosting glutamate signaling carries excitotoxicity risk.

growth factor receptor inhibitor.description: This is a broad label covering inhibitors of any receptor tyrosine kinase activated by a growth factor -- EGFR, VEGFR, PDGFR, FGFR, and others -- all of which share a common downstream logic: ligand binding drives receptor dimerization and autophosphorylation, activating RAS-MAPK and PI3K-AKT signaling that promotes cell growth and proliferation. Because many cancers depend on one or more of these receptors, growth factor receptor inhibitors are a major class of targeted cancer therapy.

growth hormone secretagogue receptor ago.description: The growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHSR), also called the ghrelin receptor, is activated by the 'hunger hormone' ghrelin to stimulate growth hormone release, appetite, and food intake. GHSR agonists mimic ghrelin's signal and are used or studied to treat conditions of poor appetite or growth hormone deficiency, such as cachexia.
growth hormone secretagogue receptor ago.typically_affects: Growth hormone release and appetite/food intake.

growth hormone secretagogue receptor inv.description: GHSR inverse agonists suppress the receptor's constitutive, ligand-independent baseline activity below its resting level, going further than a simple antagonist that only blocks ghrelin from binding. This stronger suppression of ghrelin signaling is being explored for conditions like obesity, Prader-Willi syndrome, and alcohol use disorder, where dampening appetite/reward signaling is beneficial.
growth hormone secretagogue receptor inv.typically_affects: Appetite, reward-related eating behavior, and growth hormone release.

sources.title: Small molecule ghrelin receptor inverse agonists and antagonists (PubMed)

guanylate cyclase stimulant.description: Soluble guanylate cyclase (sGC) produces the second messenger cGMP in response to nitric oxide (NO), driving smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation. sGC stimulators like riociguat work with the heme-containing form of the enzyme, sensitizing it to low levels of endogenous NO and directly stimulating it independent of NO, making them useful in conditions like pulmonary hypertension where NO signaling is impaired.

sources.title: Activation mechanism of human soluble guanylate cyclase by stimulators and activators (PMC)

guanylyl cyclase activator.description: Guanylyl cyclase activators like cinaciguat act on the same soluble guanylate cyclase enzyme but bind its heme-free form directly, activating cGMP production independent of nitric oxide entirely -- distinct from stimulators, which still require some heme/NO cooperation. This NO-independent mechanism is being explored for severe vasodilation needs, particularly when the endogenous NO system is significantly impaired.

sources.title: Activation mechanism of human soluble guanylate cyclase by stimulators and activators (PMC)

hemoglobin modulator.description: Hemoglobin modulators bind hemoglobin directly and shift its oxygen-binding affinity, altering how readily it picks up oxygen in the lungs and releases it in tissue. Voxelotor, the best-known example, increases hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and stabilizes its oxygenated shape, preventing the sickle hemoglobin polymerization that causes sickle cell disease.
hemoglobin modulator.typically_affects: Hemoglobin's oxygen affinity and, in sickle hemoglobin, polymerization behavior.

hemoglobin oxygen release stimulant.description: This label describes hemoglobin modulators that shift oxygen affinity in the opposite direction from voxelotor-like drugs, reducing hemoglobin's oxygen affinity so that more oxygen is released into hypoxic tissue. ITPP (myo-inositol trispyrophosphate) is the prototype, acting as a membrane-permeant allosteric effector that shifts the oxygen dissociation curve to increase oxygen delivery, studied in cancer and cardiovascular research.

sources.title: myo-Inositol trispyrophosphate: a novel allosteric effector of hemoglobin (PubMed)

hemozoin biocrystallization inhibitor.description: Malaria parasites digest host hemoglobin for nutrients, which releases toxic free heme; they detoxify it by crystallizing it into inert hemozoin. Hemozoin biocrystallization inhibitors, the mechanism of chloroquine and related quinoline antimalarials, bind free heme and block this crystallization, letting toxic heme accumulate and kill the parasite.
hemozoin biocrystallization inhibitor.pathway: Heme detoxification in the malaria parasite (hemozoin formation).
hemozoin biocrystallization inhibitor.typically_affects: Survival of the intraerythrocytic malaria parasite.

sources.title: Hemozoin biocrystallization in Plasmodium falciparum and the antimalarial activity of crystallization inhibitors (PubMed)

hepatocyte growth factor receptor inhibi.description: The hepatocyte growth factor receptor (HGFR) is another name for c-Met, the receptor tyrosine kinase activated by hepatocyte growth factor (HGF) to drive cell proliferation, motility, and tissue regeneration. Inhibitors of this receptor are used interchangeably with 'c-Met inhibitors' in the literature and are studied in HGF/MET-driven cancers.

histamine receptor agonist.description: Histamine receptor agonists activate one of the four histamine receptor subtypes (H1-H4), mimicking the effects of histamine released from mast cells and other tissues. Depending on the subtype engaged, this can drive allergic-type symptoms (H1), gastric acid secretion (H2), or modulate neurotransmitter release and immune cell function (H3/H4) -- these are used mainly as research tools rather than approved drugs.
histamine receptor agonist.typically_affects: Allergic responses, gastric acid secretion, and neuro-immune signaling, by subtype.

histamine receptor modulator.description: 'Histamine receptor modulator' is a broader label covering compounds that alter histamine receptor signaling without being cleanly classified as a simple agonist or antagonist -- for example, partial agonists or allosteric modulators of one of the H1-H4 subtypes. These affect the same downstream processes as histamine agonists/antagonists (allergic response, gastric acid secretion, neuro-immune signaling) but through more nuanced receptor pharmacology.
histamine receptor modulator.typically_affects: Allergic, gastric, and neuro-immune signaling, by subtype.

hypoxia inducible factor inhibitor.description: Hypoxia-inducible factors (HIFs) are transcription factors that accumulate when oxygen is low and switch on genes for angiogenesis, red blood cell production, and metabolic adaptation to hypoxia. HIF inhibitors block this transcriptional program, an approach studied in oncology since many solid tumors rely on HIF signaling to survive and grow in their low-oxygen core.
hypoxia inducible factor inhibitor.typically_affects: Angiogenesis, red blood cell production, and metabolic adaptation to low oxygen.

indicator dye.description: This is not a drug mechanism -- it's a technical label for dye compounds, such as pH or redox indicators, included in the screening library as chemical/assay reagents rather than for a specific biological target. Their 'activity' in an assay reflects a color or fluorescence change from the dye's own chemistry, not a pharmacological effect on cells.
indicator dye.pathway: None -- this is an assay/detection reagent, not a drug mechanism.
indicator dye.typically_affects: Nothing pharmacologically; used as a chemical indicator in assays.

sources.title: The Drug Repurposing Hub: a next-generation drug library and information resource (PMC)

inhaled anaesthetic.description: Inhaled general anesthetics such as isoflurane and sevoflurane induce reversible loss of consciousness through broad effects on multiple neuronal ion channels and receptors, including potentiating inhibitory GABA-A and glycine receptors while dampening excitatory NMDA and nicotinic receptors. This multi-target action produces the combination of unconsciousness, amnesia, and immobility required for surgical anesthesia.
inhaled anaesthetic.pathway: Broad modulation of neuronal ion channels (GABA-A, NMDA, and others).
inhaled anaesthetic.typically_affects: Consciousness, memory formation, and pain response during surgery.

insulin growth factor receptor inhibitor.description: This targets the same IGF-1 receptor described under 'IGF-1 inhibitor': a receptor tyrosine kinase activated by insulin-like growth factors to drive PI3K-AKT and RAS-MAPK growth signaling. Inhibitors block the receptor's kinase activity, an approach studied in cancers that depend on this growth axis.

insulin secretagogue.description: Insulin secretagogues stimulate pancreatic beta cells to release insulin independent of the normal glucose-sensing trigger; sulfonylureas, the classic example, do this by closing KATP channels to depolarize the cell and open voltage-gated calcium channels. Because this bypasses glucose-dependence, insulin secretagogues carry a higher hypoglycemia risk than newer glucose-dependent diabetes drugs.
insulin secretagogue.typically_affects: Insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells.

integrin antagonist.description: Integrins are cell-surface adhesion receptors that link cells to the extracellular matrix or to other cells, such as platelets binding fibrinogen, and also relay 'outside-in' signals affecting cell shape, migration, and survival. Integrin antagonists block this binding -- GPIIb/IIIa antagonists prevent platelet aggregation for antiplatelet therapy, while others are studied to block leukocyte adhesion in autoimmune disease or tumor cell adhesion in metastasis.
integrin antagonist.typically_affects: Platelet aggregation, leukocyte adhesion, or tumor cell adhesion, by integrin subtype.

interleukin inhibitor.description: Interleukins are a large family of cytokines that immune cells use to communicate and coordinate inflammatory and immune responses. Interleukin inhibitors block a specific interleukin or its receptor, such as IL-6, IL-17, or IL-1 inhibitors, reducing that cytokine's downstream inflammatory signaling -- a major mechanism behind modern biologic drugs for autoimmune and inflammatory diseases.
interleukin inhibitor.typically_affects: Inflammatory and immune signaling, specific to the targeted interleukin.

leukocyte elastase inhibitor.description: Leukocyte (neutrophil) elastase is the specific elastase released by neutrophils during inflammation to help them migrate through tissue and kill pathogens, but excess activity degrades lung elastin and drives emphysema. Leukocyte elastase inhibitors, including the natural inhibitor alpha-1 antitrypsin, block this enzyme to protect lung tissue from neutrophil-driven damage.
leukocyte elastase inhibitor.typically_affects: Lung tissue elasticity and neutrophil-mediated tissue remodeling.

leukotriene receptor antagonist.description: Leukotrienes are lipid inflammatory mediators, derived from arachidonic acid via the lipoxygenase pathway (a parallel route to the COX/prostaglandin pathway), that drive bronchoconstriction, mucus secretion, and airway inflammation in asthma. Leukotriene receptor antagonists like montelukast block their receptors to reduce these asthmatic and allergic symptoms.
leukotriene receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Airway inflammation, bronchoconstriction, and allergic symptoms.

lysophosphatidic acid receptor antagonis.description: Lysophosphatidic acid (LPA) is a lipid signaling molecule that activates a family of G-protein-coupled receptors (LPA1-6) to drive cell proliferation, migration, and, notably, fibrotic scarring. LPA receptor antagonists block this signaling, an approach being studied to treat pulmonary and other organ fibrosis.
lysophosphatidic acid receptor antagonis.typically_affects: Cell proliferation, migration, and fibrotic tissue remodeling.

lysophospholipid receptor antagonist.description: Lysophospholipid receptors are a broader receptor family that includes both the LPA receptors and the sphingosine-1-phosphate (S1P) receptors, all activated by lipid-derived signaling molecules to control cell migration, vascular biology, and immune cell trafficking. Antagonists of this receptor family block one or more of these lipid-GPCR signals, depending on which specific lysophospholipid receptor is targeted.
lysophospholipid receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Cell migration, vascular biology, and immune cell trafficking.

matrix metalloprotease inhibitor.description: Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) are zinc-dependent enzymes that degrade extracellular matrix proteins like collagen, a process essential for normal tissue remodeling but also exploited by cancer cells to invade surrounding tissue and metastasize. MMP inhibitors block this degradation, an approach studied, with mixed clinical success, to limit tumor invasion and, separately, to slow tissue destruction in arthritis.
matrix metalloprotease inhibitor.typically_affects: Tissue remodeling and cancer cell invasion/metastasis.

membrane permeability inhibitor.description: This is a broad mechanistic label for compounds that stabilize cell or organelle membranes against damage-induced permeability changes, rather than a single defined molecular target -- for example, protecting mitochondrial or plasma membrane integrity against stress-induced leakage. Because membrane permeabilization is a common step in both necrotic and apoptotic cell death, agents in this class are generally studied for their cell/tissue-protective, anti-injury effects.
membrane permeability inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell membrane stability under stress; protection against necrotic or apoptotic membrane damage.

microtubule inhibitor.description: Microtubule inhibitor is a broad category covering any compound that disrupts normal microtubule dynamics, whether by blocking new polymerization (destabilizers, like vinca alkaloids) or by locking microtubules in place and preventing their normal disassembly (stabilizers, like taxanes). Because dividing cells rely on precisely controlled microtubule dynamics to build the mitotic spindle, both mechanisms halt cell division and are widely used in cancer chemotherapy.
