microtubule inhibitor.pathway: Microtubule dynamics in the cytoskeleton and mitotic spindle.

sources.title: Discovery and development of tubulin inhibitors - Wikipedia

microtubule stabilizing agent.description: Unlike tubulin polymerization inhibitors, microtubule stabilizing agents such as paclitaxel bind assembled microtubules and prevent them from disassembling, effectively freezing the microtubule network. This blocks the dynamic remodeling that mitotic spindles require to properly segregate chromosomes, arresting cells in mitosis and triggering death of rapidly dividing cancer cells.

mitochondrial DNA polymerase inhibitor.description: DNA polymerase gamma (Pol gamma) is the sole enzyme responsible for replicating mitochondrial DNA. Certain nucleoside-analog drugs, originally designed to inhibit viral reverse transcriptase such as older HIV drugs like zidovudine, can also inhibit Pol gamma off-target, depleting mitochondrial DNA and causing the mitochondrial toxicity (myopathy, neuropathy) seen with some antiviral regimens.
mitochondrial DNA polymerase inhibitor.typically_affects: Mitochondrial DNA content and, downstream, mitochondrial function in tissues with high energy demand (muscle, nerve).

sources.title: A mechanistic view of human mitochondrial DNA polymerase gamma (PMC)

monoamine oxidase inhibitor.description: Monoamine oxidase (MAO) breaks down monoamine neurotransmitters -- serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine -- in nerve terminals. MAO inhibitors (MAOIs) block this breakdown, raising synaptic monoamine levels and were among the first antidepressant drug classes, though they've largely been replaced clinically by SSRIs due to drug/food interaction risks.
monoamine oxidase inhibitor.typically_affects: Serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine levels in the brain.

mucolytic agent.description: Mucolytic agents break down the disulfide bonds that cross-link mucus glycoproteins, reducing mucus viscosity so it can be cleared more easily from the airways by coughing or ciliary action. Acetylcysteine is the classic example, used for conditions with thick, difficult-to-clear mucus such as cystic fibrosis and chronic bronchitis.

neprilysin inhibitor.description: Neprilysin degrades natriuretic peptides (ANP, BNP) -- hormones that promote vasodilation and sodium excretion to relieve pressure on a failing heart -- along with enkephalins (neprilysin is the same enzyme as 'enkephalinase'). Neprilysin inhibitors like sacubitril block this breakdown, boosting natriuretic peptide levels, and are used, combined with an angiotensin receptor blocker, as a heart failure treatment.
neprilysin inhibitor.typically_affects: Vasodilation and sodium excretion via natriuretic peptide signaling; cardiac load in heart failure.

niacinamide phosphoribosyltransferase in.description: Nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT) catalyzes the rate-limiting step of the NAD+ salvage pathway, which most cells, and especially fast-growing cancer cells, rely on to replenish NAD+ for energy metabolism and DNA repair. NAMPT inhibitors like FK866 block this step, depleting NAD+ and starving cancer cells of the bioenergetic support they need to proliferate.
niacinamide phosphoribosyltransferase in.typically_affects: Cellular NAD+ levels and, downstream, energy metabolism and proliferation.

sources.title: Inhibition of nicotinamide phosphoribosyltransferase (NAMPT) with OT-82 (PMC)

nociceptin/orphanin FQ receptor agonist.description: The nociceptin/orphanin FQ receptor (NOP) is structurally related to classical opioid receptors but is activated by its own distinct peptide ligand rather than by endorphins or exogenous opioids, and produces largely opioid-independent effects on pain, mood, and reward. NOP agonists activate this receptor and are being studied for pain and anxiety, notably as non-addictive alternatives to classical opioids.
nociceptin/orphanin FQ receptor agonist.typically_affects: Pain modulation, anxiety, and reward circuits, largely independent of classical opioid signaling.

nociceptin/orphanin FQ receptor antagoni.description: NOP receptor antagonists block the same receptor, preventing nociceptin/orphanin FQ from signaling. Because NOP activation can oppose some of the rewarding effects of classical opioids, NOP antagonists are studied for depression and substance use disorders, where boosting reward-circuit activity is the therapeutic goal.

non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inh.description: HIV reverse transcriptase converts the virus's RNA genome into DNA so it can integrate into the host cell. Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) bind an allosteric pocket on the enzyme, distinct from the active site targeted by nucleoside analogs, and lock it in an inactive shape, blocking this essential step of the HIV life cycle.

opioid receptor agonist.description: Opioid receptors (mu, delta, kappa) are G-protein-coupled receptors normally activated by the body's own endorphins/enkephalins to dampen pain signaling in the spinal cord and brain. Opioid receptor agonists, from morphine to synthetic analgesics, mimic this signal for pain relief, though mu-receptor activation also drives the euphoria and respiratory depression responsible for opioid misuse and overdose risk.
opioid receptor agonist.typically_affects: Pain perception, mood/reward, and respiratory drive.

opioid receptor antagonist.description: Opioid receptor antagonists such as naloxone and naltrexone bind the same receptors without activating them, blocking endogenous or exogenous opioids from producing their effect. Naloxone's rapid reversal of opioid receptor binding is the basis for its use in reversing opioid overdose.
opioid receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Pain perception, mood/reward, and respiratory drive.

oxytocin receptor agonist.description: The oxytocin receptor is a G-protein-coupled receptor activated by oxytocin to drive uterine contraction during labor and milk ejection during breastfeeding, alongside roles in social bonding behavior. Oxytocin receptor agonists mimic this signal and are used clinically to induce or augment labor and to control postpartum bleeding.
oxytocin receptor agonist.typically_affects: Uterine contraction and milk ejection reflex.

phosphatase inhibitor.description: Phosphatases remove phosphate groups from proteins, reversing the effect of kinases and, together with them, forming the on/off switches of most cell signaling pathways. Phosphatase inhibitors block this removal step -- for example, calcineurin inhibitors like tacrolimus block a calcium-dependent phosphatase required for T-cell activation, the basis for their use as immunosuppressants.
phosphatase inhibitor.typically_affects: Varies by specific phosphatase target; broadly, signal transduction.

phosphoinositide dependent kinase inhibi.description: 3-phosphoinositide-dependent kinase 1 (PDK1) is a master kinase recruited to the membrane by PI3K-generated lipids, where it activates AKT and other AGC-family kinases central to cell growth and survival. PDK1 inhibitors block this activation step, an approach studied in cancers with hyperactive PI3K-AKT signaling.

sources.title: Multiple implications of 3-phosphoinositide-dependent protein kinase 1 in human cancer (PMC)

phospholipase inhibitor.description: Phospholipase enzymes, such as phospholipase A2, cleave membrane phospholipids to release arachidonic acid, the starting material for prostaglandin and leukotriene inflammatory signaling. Phospholipase inhibitors block this release upstream of both the COX and lipoxygenase pathways -- corticosteroids work partly through this mechanism, inducing a protein (lipocortin/annexin A1) that inhibits phospholipase A2.
phospholipase inhibitor.pathway: Arachidonic acid release (phospholipase A2 activity), upstream of prostaglandin/leukotriene synthesis.

porcupine inhibitor.description: Porcupine (PORCN) is an enzyme in the endoplasmic reticulum that attaches a lipid group to Wnt signaling proteins, a modification required for Wnt to be secreted and to bind its receptor. Porcupine inhibitors like LGK974 block this lipidation step, preventing Wnt secretion altogether and shutting down Wnt/beta-catenin signaling in Wnt-driven cancers.
porcupine inhibitor.pathway: Wnt ligand lipidation and secretion (Porcupine/PORCN activity).
porcupine inhibitor.typically_affects: Wnt/beta-catenin signaling and Wnt-dependent cell proliferation.

sources.title: Porcupine Inhibitor LGK974 Downregulates the Wnt Signaling Pathway (PMC)

potassium channel activator.description: Potassium channel activators, also called potassium channel openers, increase potassium outflow through channels like KATP, hyperpolarizing the cell membrane and reducing excitability. Drugs like nicorandil and diazoxide use this mechanism to relax vascular smooth muscle for angina/hypertension or, in the case of diazoxide, to suppress excess insulin secretion.
potassium channel activator.typically_affects: Vascular smooth muscle tone and, for KATP-selective agents, insulin secretion.

potassium channel agonist.description: This label overlaps closely with 'potassium channel activator': both describe compounds that increase potassium channel opening/conductance to hyperpolarize the cell membrane. The terminology varies by source -- 'opener'/'activator' is more common for the KATP-targeting drug class (e.g. minoxidil, nicorandil), while 'agonist' is sometimes used more loosely for the same mechanism.
potassium channel agonist.typically_affects: Vascular smooth muscle tone and cellular excitability.

prostacyclin analog.description: Prostacyclin (PGI2) is a prostaglandin produced by vascular endothelium that causes vasodilation and inhibits platelet aggregation. Prostacyclin analogs such as epoprostenol and treprostinil mimic this signal and are used to treat pulmonary arterial hypertension, where they relax the pulmonary blood vessels and reduce the excess clotting risk in that disease.

prostaglandin inhibitor.description: Prostaglandin inhibitors reduce the production or action of prostaglandins, the arachidonic-acid-derived lipid mediators involved in inflammation, pain, fever, and vascular tone, overlapping with the mechanism of cyclooxygenase inhibitors, though the label here may also include downstream prostaglandin receptor or synthase-specific inhibitors. The net effect is reduced inflammatory and pain signaling wherever prostaglandins are the driver.
prostaglandin inhibitor.pathway: Arachidonic acid metabolism / prostaglandin synthesis or signaling.
prostaglandin inhibitor.typically_affects: Inflammation, pain signaling, and vascular tone.

prostanoid receptor agonist.description: Prostanoid receptor agonists activate the family of prostaglandin/thromboxane receptors (EP1-4, DP, FP, IP, TP) directly, mimicking the diverse effects these lipid mediators have on inflammation, smooth muscle tone, and platelet activity depending on which receptor subtype is engaged. FP receptor agonists like latanoprost, for example, are used to lower eye pressure in glaucoma by increasing fluid outflow.
prostanoid receptor agonist.typically_affects: Smooth muscle tone, ocular fluid dynamics, and platelet activity, depending on receptor subtype.

sources.title: Prostanoid receptors -- IUPHAR/BPS Guide to Pharmacology

protease inhibitor.description: Protease inhibitors block enzymes that cleave proteins at specific sites -- a mechanism used across very different contexts, from HIV protease inhibitors that block viral polyprotein processing (preventing new infectious virus particles from maturing) to protease inhibitors targeting host enzymes involved in digestion, coagulation, or inflammation. The shared mechanism is direct occupation of the protease's active site to block its cleavage activity.
protease inhibitor.typically_affects: Varies by target protease -- viral maturation, digestion, coagulation, or inflammatory signaling.

proteasome inhibitor.description: The proteasome is the cell's main machinery for degrading unneeded or misfolded proteins, tagged for destruction by ubiquitin. Proteasome inhibitors like bortezomib block this degradation, causing toxic protein buildup that is especially lethal to cancer cells like multiple myeloma plasma cells, which produce large amounts of protein and are highly dependent on proteasome function.
proteasome inhibitor.typically_affects: Intracellular protein turnover; particularly lethal to protein-secretion-heavy cancer cells.

protein tyrosine kinase inhibitor.description: This is a broad category covering inhibitors of any tyrosine kinase, receptor or non-receptor, enzymes that phosphorylate tyrosine residues on target proteins to relay growth and survival signals. Because aberrant tyrosine kinase activity drives many cancers, this class includes some of the most successful targeted cancer therapies, spanning specific kinases like EGFR and BCR-ABL already described individually in this dataset.
protein tyrosine kinase inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell growth, proliferation, and survival signaling.

purine antagonist.description: Purine antagonists are antimetabolites that structurally resemble purine bases (adenine, guanine) and get incorporated into the nucleotide synthesis pathway, where they block normal purine metabolism and DNA/RNA synthesis. Because this most affects rapidly dividing cells, purine antagonists like 6-mercaptopurine are used as chemotherapy and immunosuppressants.
purine antagonist.typically_affects: DNA/RNA synthesis in rapidly dividing cells.

purinergic receptor antagonist.description: Purinergic receptors -- P1 adenosine receptors and P2 ATP/ADP receptors -- respond to extracellular nucleotides and nucleosides released during cell stress, injury, or platelet activation. Purinergic receptor antagonists block this signaling; P2Y12 antagonists like clopidogrel, for example, prevent ADP-driven platelet aggregation and are widely used as antiplatelet drugs.
purinergic receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Platelet aggregation, inflammation, and pain signaling, depending on receptor subtype.

rho associated kinase inhibitor.description: Rho-associated kinase (ROCK) acts downstream of the small GTPase RhoA to regulate the actin cytoskeleton, controlling smooth muscle contraction, cell shape, and migration. ROCK inhibitors like fasudil block this signaling to relax vascular and other smooth muscle, and are used or studied for glaucoma, vasospasm, and other conditions involving excess ROCK-driven contraction.
rho associated kinase inhibitor.typically_affects: Smooth muscle contraction, cell shape, and migration.

ribonucleotide reductase inhibitor.description: Ribonucleotide reductase converts ribonucleotides into the deoxyribonucleotides required for DNA synthesis and repair, making it a rate-limiting step for any dividing cell. Ribonucleotide reductase inhibitors like hydroxyurea block this conversion, starving cells of DNA building blocks -- used in chemotherapy and in some antiviral and antimalarial contexts.
ribonucleotide reductase inhibitor.typically_affects: DNA synthesis and repair in dividing cells.

ribosomal protein inhibitor.description: This is a broad label for compounds that bind directly to ribosomal proteins, rather than ribosomal RNA, to block translation, overlapping mechanistically with the protein synthesis and 30S ribosomal subunit inhibitors described elsewhere in this dataset. Many antibiotics work by this general route, exploiting structural differences between bacterial and human ribosomal proteins for selective toxicity.
ribosomal protein inhibitor.typically_affects: Bacterial (or, for non-selective agents, host) protein synthesis.

serine protease inhibitor.description: Serine proteases use a reactive serine residue in their active site to cleave target proteins, and this enzyme family includes digestive enzymes, clotting factors, and immune proteases. Serine protease inhibitors block this active site directly -- camostat, for example, inhibits serine proteases involved in viral entry and pancreatic conditions, while natural serine protease inhibitors (serpins) regulate coagulation and inflammation in the body.
serine protease inhibitor.typically_affects: Varies by target -- coagulation, digestion, inflammation, or viral entry, depending on the specific protease.

serotonin receptor agonist.description: Serotonin receptors (5-HT1-7, mostly G-protein-coupled except the ligand-gated 5-HT3) mediate serotonin's wide-ranging effects on mood, appetite, nausea, and vascular tone. Serotonin receptor agonists mimic this signaling at a specific receptor subtype -- 5-HT1B/1D agonists (triptans), for example, constrict cranial blood vessels to abort migraines, while other subtypes are targeted for depression or nausea.
serotonin receptor agonist.typically_affects: Mood, appetite, nausea, and vascular tone, depending on receptor subtype.

serotonin receptor antagonist.description: Serotonin receptor antagonists block the same receptor family -- 5-HT3 antagonists like ondansetron, for example, block serotonin's role in triggering nausea and vomiting, making them standard antiemetics, while other subtype-selective antagonists are used for migraine prevention or as components of atypical antipsychotics.
serotonin receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Nausea/vomiting reflex, mood, and vascular tone, depending on receptor subtype.

serotonin reuptake inhibitor.description: Serotonin reuptake inhibitors block the serotonin transporter (SERT) that normally clears serotonin from the synapse after release, prolonging its availability to postsynaptic receptors. This is the mechanism of SSRIs, the most widely prescribed class of antidepressants, based on the theory that boosting synaptic serotonin helps relieve depression and anxiety.
serotonin reuptake inhibitor.typically_affects: Synaptic serotonin availability; mood and anxiety regulation.

smoothened receptor antagonist.description: Smoothened (SMO) is a G-protein-coupled-like receptor that transmits Hedgehog pathway signaling once released from suppression by the Hedgehog receptor Patched; excess Hedgehog/SMO signaling drives basal cell carcinoma and some other cancers. Smoothened antagonists like vismodegib bind and lock SMO in an inactive state, shutting down this signaling in Hedgehog-pathway-driven tumors.
smoothened receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Cell proliferation in Hedgehog-pathway-driven tissue and tumors.

somatostatin receptor agonist.description: Somatostatin receptors are G-protein-coupled receptors that, when activated by somatostatin, broadly inhibit hormone secretion throughout the body, including growth hormone, insulin, and glucagon. Somatostatin receptor agonists like octreotide mimic this suppressive signal and are used to treat conditions of hormone excess, such as acromegaly and certain neuroendocrine tumors.
somatostatin receptor agonist.typically_affects: Hormone secretion (growth hormone, insulin, glucagon, and others).

sphingosine 1-phosphate receptor antagon.description: S1P receptor antagonists block the same receptor family described for S1P receptor agonists elsewhere in this dataset, but rather than trapping lymphocytes in lymph nodes, blocking the receptor can prevent S1P from directing normal lymphocyte egress and vascular signaling -- the specific therapeutic consequence depends heavily on which S1P receptor subtype and cell type is affected.

sterol demethylase inhibitor.description: Sterol 14-alpha demethylase, a cytochrome P450 enzyme, catalyzes an essential step in synthesizing ergosterol, the fungal equivalent of cholesterol that maintains fungal cell membrane integrity. Sterol demethylase inhibitors -- the azole class of antifungals such as fluconazole -- block this enzyme, depleting ergosterol and disrupting the fungal cell membrane.

sulfonylurea.description: Sulfonylureas are a specific drug class that close KATP channels on pancreatic beta cells, depolarizing them and triggering insulin release independent of blood glucose level -- the same underlying mechanism described under 'ATP channel blocker' and 'insulin secretagogue.' They were among the first oral diabetes drugs but carry a meaningful hypoglycemia risk because of this glucose-independent action.
sulfonylurea.typically_affects: Insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells.

synthetic estrogen.description: Synthetic estrogens are lab-made compounds, such as ethinylestradiol, that activate estrogen receptors just as the body's natural estrogen does, but are chemically modified for properties like oral bioavailability or a longer half-life. They're widely used in hormonal contraceptives and menopausal hormone therapy.
synthetic estrogen.typically_affects: Reproductive tissue growth, menstrual cycle regulation, and secondary sex characteristics.

thromboxane synthase inhibitor.description: Thromboxane synthase converts a prostaglandin intermediate into thromboxane A2, a potent promoter of platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction. Thromboxane synthase inhibitors block this final synthesis step, reducing platelet clumping without blocking the entire cyclooxygenase pathway the way aspirin-like drugs do.

tissue transglutaminase inhibitor.description: Tissue transglutaminase (TG2) crosslinks proteins by forming bonds between glutamine and lysine residues, a reaction implicated in fibrosis, neurodegeneration, and, notably, celiac disease, where TG2 modifies gluten peptides in a way that triggers autoimmune attack. TG2 inhibitors block this crosslinking activity, studied both for celiac disease and for fibrotic and neurodegenerative conditions.
tissue transglutaminase inhibitor.typically_affects: Fibrotic tissue remodeling; in celiac disease, autoimmune gluten-peptide recognition.

transglutaminase inhibitor.description: Transglutaminase inhibitors block this same family of crosslinking enzymes more broadly, not just the tissue/TG2 isoform, reducing protein-protein crosslinking wherever a given transglutaminase isoform is active -- relevant across the same fibrotic, autoimmune, and neurodegenerative contexts described for tissue transglutaminase inhibitors.
transglutaminase inhibitor.typically_affects: Fibrotic tissue remodeling and protein aggregation.

triacylglycerol lipase inhibitor.description: Triacylglycerol lipases, including pancreatic lipase, break down dietary fat into absorbable free fatty acids in the gut. Inhibitors like orlistat block this enzyme, preventing roughly a third of ingested fat from being digested and absorbed, which is used as a weight-management therapy.

tumor necrosis factor production inhibit.description: Tumor necrosis factor (TNF-alpha) is a central pro-inflammatory cytokine that drives much of the tissue damage in autoimmune diseases like rheumatoid arthritis and inflammatory bowel disease. TNF production inhibitors block the intracellular signaling needed to synthesize and secrete TNF, as opposed to TNF-neutralizing antibody biologics that mop up TNF after it's released, reducing inflammation upstream.
tumor necrosis factor production inhibit.typically_affects: Inflammatory signaling in autoimmune and chronic inflammatory disease.

tyrosine kinase inhibitor.description: Tyrosine kinase inhibitors are a broad drug class that blocks tyrosine kinase enzymes, receptor or non-receptor, at their ATP-binding site, shutting down the phosphorylation-driven growth and survival signaling many cancers depend on. This class includes many of the specific kinase inhibitors described individually elsewhere in this dataset (EGFR, BCR-ABL, VEGFR, and others) and represents one of the largest and most successful categories of targeted cancer therapy.
tyrosine kinase inhibitor.typically_affects: Cell growth, proliferation, and survival signaling.

tyrosine phosphatase inhibitor.description: Protein tyrosine phosphatases remove phosphate groups that tyrosine kinases add, acting as the 'off switch' counterpart in tyrosine kinase signaling. Tyrosine phosphatase inhibitors block this removal, prolonging tyrosine phosphorylation signals -- of interest both as research tools and, for specific phosphatases like PTP1B, a negative regulator of insulin signaling, as a potential diabetes/obesity drug target.
tyrosine phosphatase inhibitor.typically_affects: Duration/strength of tyrosine kinase signaling; insulin signaling for select targets like PTP1B.

ubiquitin specific protease inhibitor.description: Ubiquitin-specific proteases (USPs) are the largest family of deubiquitinating enzymes (DUBs), removing ubiquitin tags from proteins and thereby rescuing them from proteasomal degradation. USP inhibitors block this 'rescue' step, promoting degradation of proteins, including some oncoproteins, that would otherwise escape the proteasome -- an approach being explored as an alternative or complement to direct proteasome inhibition in cancer.
ubiquitin specific protease inhibitor.typically_affects: Protein stability and turnover via the ubiquitin-proteasome system.

uricosuric blocker.description: This label describes drugs that block the URAT1 transporter in the kidney, which normally reabsorbs filtered uric acid back into the blood. By blocking URAT1, as benzbromarone or lesinurad do, these drugs prevent that reabsorption, increasing uric acid excretion in the urine -- the standard uricosuric strategy for lowering blood urate levels in gout.
uricosuric blocker.typically_affects: Blood uric acid levels via increased renal excretion.

sources.title: Molecular mechanism of drug inhibition of URAT1 (Nature Communications)

vasopressin receptor antagonist.description: Vasopressin (antidiuretic hormone) acts on V1 receptors, causing vascular smooth muscle constriction, and V2 receptors, driving kidney water reabsorption, to regulate blood pressure and fluid balance. Vasopressin receptor antagonists ('vaptans') block these receptors -- V2 antagonists like tolvaptan promote water excretion without sodium loss, used to treat fluid overload conditions like hyponatremia and heart failure.
vasopressin receptor antagonist.typically_affects: Renal water reabsorption and vascular tone.

vitamin K antagonist.description: Vitamin K antagonists like warfarin block the vitamin K epoxide reductase enzyme needed to recycle vitamin K, which the liver requires to activate several clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X). By depleting active vitamin K, these drugs reduce the liver's ability to produce functional clotting factors, providing anticoagulation -- one of the oldest and most widely used oral anticoagulant mechanisms.
vitamin K antagonist.pathway: Vitamin K cycle (hepatic synthesis of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X).
vitamin K antagonist.typically_affects: Blood coagulation via clotting factor synthesis.
